content.problemStatement:
Salmon has time-travelled to the dying days of the Roman Empire, and needs to travel to Rome.

There are NNN cities in the Roman Empire; these are numbered from 000 to N‚ąí1N-1N‚ąí1. City 000 is Rome. There are also MMM unidirectional roads between some of these NNN cities. The iii-th road is from city AiA_iAi‚Äč to city BiB_iBi‚Äč, and has a length of WiW_iWi‚Äč.

As the old saying goes, "All roads lead to Rome". In particular, starting at a city and following an arbitrary sequence of roads, one will eventually reach Rome and terminate. Note that there are no directed cycles in this graph. In other words, it is not possible to leave a city and then return to it later.

In addition, to prevent the Empire from becoming too fractured, the roads were built such that the largest set of cities such that all cities in the set are pairwise unreachable from each other has size ‚Č§10\leq 10‚Č§10. In other words, it is guaranteed that in any set of 111111 or more vertices, there will exist at least two vertices uuu and vvv, such that it is possible to travel from uuu to vvv in the original graph.

Salmon will make a journey from each city towards Rome. Each journey can be represented as a sequence of cities, SSS, such that the first integer is the origin city and the last integer is city 0, Rome. Note that SSS may have any size.

Salmon needs to travel between every two consecutive cities on his journey. It is not necessary that the cities are directly connected, however, the latter city must be reachable from the earlier one. Roman authorities now mandate that to travel from city XXX to YYY, one must hire soldiers for protection in city XXX. As nowadays journeys near Rome are more dangerous, the number of soldiers to hire is equal to K‚ąídYK-d_YK‚ąídY‚Äč where dYd_YdY‚Äč represents the length of the longest path from YYY to Rome, and KKK is a constant. The cost of hiring a single soldier in city XXX is cXc_XcX‚Äč. In addition, Salmon will have to pay an entry tax tYt_YtY‚Äč upon arrival in city YYY, to enter. Notice that Salmon is not required to pay the entry fee for the city he starts in (the origin city), and that he only has to pay the entry fee for cities in the sequence SSS.

Given that Salmon chooses his journeys optimally, what is the minimum cost of travelling from every city from 1‚Ä¶N‚ąí11 \dots N-11‚Ä¶N‚ąí1 to Rome?

###Input:


The first line will contain three integers: NNN, MMM, and KKK.
The second line will contain NNN integers: c0c1‚Ä¶cN‚ąí1c_0 c_1 \dots c_{N-1}c0‚Äčc1‚Äč‚Ä¶cN‚ąí1‚Äč
The third line will contain NNN integers: t0t1‚Ä¶tN‚ąí1t_0 t_1 \dots t_{N-1}t0‚Äčt1‚Äč‚Ä¶tN‚ąí1‚Äč
The next MMM lines will contain three integers each: uiviwiu_i v_i w_iui‚Äčvi‚Äčwi‚Äč, representing that there is an road from uiu_iui‚Äč to viv_ivi‚Äč of length wiw_iwi‚Äč.
Output:Output N‚ąí1N-1N‚ąí1 space-separated integers: the iii-th integer is the minimum cost to travel from city iii to Rome (city 0).
constraints: dmax‚Č§K‚Č§109d_{max} \leq K \leq 10^9dmax‚Äč‚Č§K‚Č§109, where dmaxd_{max}dmax‚Äč is the highest value of did_idi‚Äč across all cities.
The road network forms a connected Directed Acyclic Graph, with node 000 as the sole sink.
The largest set of cities such that all cities are pairwise unreachable from each other has size ‚Č§10\leq 10‚Č§10.
Subtask 2 [15 points]: M=N‚ąí1M = N-1M=N‚ąí1; ui=i+1u_i=i+1ui‚Äč=i+1, vi=iv_i=ivi‚Äč=i for all iii

`ExplanationSalmon travels directly from city 1 to Rome (city 0). He has to hire 5‚ąí0=55-0 = 55‚ąí0=5 soldiers. The hiring-cost of each soldier in city 1 is 222. So, he has to spend 101010 on soldier salaries. In addition, he has to pay a tax of 333 to enter city 0, for a total of 131313.
